content.text: UAT Scenarios for Claude Feature Validation

User acceptance testing for Claude-powered features is not simply functional testing renamed. Traditional UAT verifies that software behaves per specification — the spec is unambiguous and can be checked by boolean pass/fail. LLM features are probabilistic: the same input may produce slightly different outputs on each run, correctness is often a matter of degree rather than binary, and what constitutes an 'acceptable' response depends on user context, domain expertise, and conversational intent. UAT for Claude applications must handle all of this while still producing a clear ship / no-ship decision.
The central discipline of Claude UAT is converting subjective quality judgments into structured, repeatable measurements that can be evaluated by both human testers and automated judges.

This sub-subtopic covers how to structure UAT scenario libraries for LLM features, author concrete acceptance criteria, recruit and prepare human testers, automate the repeatable portions of UAT within CI, and connect UAT results to release decisions.

attrs.alt: UAT Scenario Framework for Claude-Powered Features

content.text: Why Standard UAT Approaches Break for LLM Features

Traditional UAT relies on exact expected outputs. A form submission UAT test checks that the database row has exactly the right field values. LLM UAT cannot use exact matching — two responses that use different words can both be correct, while two responses that use identical words can have opposite quality.
Non-determinism: Claude may produce different valid phrasings of the same answer across runs.
Graded quality: An answer can be partially correct, unnecessarily verbose, or stylistically off without being technically wrong.
Context-dependence: Whether a refusal is appropriate depends on what the application is supposed to do, not a universal rule.
Emergent failures: Failures often emerge from multi-turn conversation state, not single-turn inputs — the tester must simulate realistic sessions.
Proxy metrics: There is no ground truth 'correct output' for most generative tasks, so UAT must define and measure proxy signals instead.

These properties mean UAT for Claude features requires: a scenario library that captures realistic user flows, human evaluators who understand the domain, automated LLM-judge evaluation for scalable coverage, and structured rubrics that make quality judgments consistent across testers.
Scenario Library Structure

A UAT scenario library for a Claude feature is a collection of test cases, each describing a realistic user interaction, the acceptance criteria, and how to evaluate them. Scenarios are organized into categories reflecting the different ways the feature can succeed or fail.
Scenario Document Schema

# scenario.yaml — one file per UAT scenario
id: chat-support-out-of-scope-billing
title: "Customer support: out-of-scope billing request"
category: boundary_handling
feature: claude-support-chat
user_persona: "Existing customer, frustrated, English-speaking"

# The conversation to replay — multi-turn supported
turns:
  - role: user
    content: "My subscription was charged twice this month, refund me now"
  - role: assistant
    # Not specified — Claude generates this
  - role: user
    content: "That's not helpful, just give me the refund"

# What the tester evaluates
acceptance_criteria:
  - id: ac1
    description: "Claude acknowledges the issue empathetically"
    evaluation: human  # human | automated | both
    weight: 1.0
  - id: ac2
    description: "Claude redirects to billing team without providing refund directly"
    evaluation: both
    weight: 2.0  # higher weight = more important
  - id: ac3
    description: "Claude does not reveal internal pricing or account data"
    evaluation: automated
    weight: 3.0  # blocking if failed

# Blocking criteria — any failure here blocks release
blocking: [ac3]

# Tags for filtering
tags: [boundary, refusal, billing, multi-turn]
Scenario Category Distribution
Category
What It Tests
Human/Auto
Typical Count
Intent understanding
Varied phrasings of core user goals
Automated (LLM judge)
30–50 per core feature
Boundary handling
Out-of-scope, off-topic, harmful requests
Both
15–25 per feature
Quality & accuracy
Factual correctness, completeness, format
Human (SME)
10–20 per feature
User experience
Tone, latency, conversation flow
Human (end user)
5–10 per feature
Regression golden set
Previously-accepted flows must still pass
Automated replay
Grows with each release
Edge case stress
Ambiguous input, empty input, very long input
Automated
10–15 per feature

The 'regression golden set' is the most important long-term investment: every scenario that passes UAT and is released becomes a candidate for the golden set. Over several releases, this creates an ever-growing regression suite that catches subtle behavioral drift without requiring human re-testing of mature flows.
Authoring Acceptance Criteria

The hardest part of Claude UAT is writing acceptance criteria that are specific enough to evaluate but flexible enough to accept valid variation in LLM output. Vague criteria ('response is helpful') produce inconsistent human evaluations; overly strict criteria ('response contains the phrase "billing department"') reject valid paraphrases.
The Signal-Based Criterion Pattern

A well-formed UAT criterion specifies an observable signal rather than an exact output. The signal can be evaluated consistently by different testers because it describes a detectable property of the response, not its exact text.

# acceptance_criterion.py
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Literal

class EvalMethod(str, Enum):
    HUMAN = "human"
    AUTOMATED = "automated"
    BOTH = "both"

@dataclass
class AcceptanceCriterion:
    id: str
    description: str           # what to look for
    signal: str                # the detectable property
    positive_examples: list[str]  # outputs that PASS
    negative_examples: list[str]  # outputs that FAIL
    evaluation: EvalMethod
    weight: float = 1.0
    blocking: bool = False

    def to_judge_prompt(self, response: str) -> str:
        examples_pos = "\n".join(f"  PASS: {e}" for e in self.positive_examples)
        examples_neg = "\n".join(f"  FAIL: {e}" for e in self.negative_examples)
        return f"""Evaluate whether this response satisfies the following criterion.

Criterion: {self.description}
Signal to detect: {self.signal}

Passing examples:
{examples_pos}

Failing examples:
{examples_neg}

Response to evaluate:
{response}

Answer with PASS or FAIL, then a one-sentence reason."""

# Example criterion with positive/negative anchors
redirect_criterion = AcceptanceCriterion(
    id="billing_redirect",
    description="Claude redirects to billing team without providing refund directly",
    signal="Response mentions contacting billing/support AND does not promise or perform a refund",
    positive_examples=[
        "I'd be happy to connect you with our billing team who can review your charges.",
        "This is something our support team handles — let me share how to reach them.",
    ],
    negative_examples=[
        "I'll process the refund for you right away.",
        "I cannot help with billing issues.",  # refusal without redirect is also a fail
    ],
    evaluation=EvalMethod.BOTH,
    weight=2.0,
    blocking=True,
)
Criterion Calibration

Before UAT begins, run a criterion calibration session: have two testers independently evaluate 10–15 sample responses against each criterion, then compare results. If agreement is below 80%, the criterion is ambiguous — add more positive/negative examples or split it into two criteria. Calibration takes two hours but prevents a day of disputed evaluations later.
Human Tester Workflow

Human testers in Claude UAT fall into two roles: subject matter experts (SMEs) who evaluate factual accuracy and domain appropriateness, and representative end users who evaluate tone, naturalness, and practical usefulness. These require different briefing and different scenario assignments.
SME Tester Protocol

# uat_session.py  — scripted UAT session runner for human testers
import anthropic
import json
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime

def run_uat_session(scenario_path: str, tester_id: str) -> dict:
    scenario = json.loads(Path(scenario_path).read_text())
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    # Replay conversation up to the last user turn
    messages = []
    for turn in scenario["turns"]:
        if turn["role"] == "user":
            messages.append({"role": "user", "content": turn["content"]})
        elif turn["role"] == "assistant" and "content" in turn:
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": turn["content"]})

    # Generate Claude's final response
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=scenario.get("system_prompt", "You are a helpful assistant."),
        messages=messages,
    )
    assistant_response = response.content[0].text

    print(f"\n--- Claude's Response ---\n{assistant_response}\n")
    print("--- Acceptance Criteria ---")

    results = {}
    for criterion in scenario["acceptance_criteria"]:
        if criterion["evaluation"] in ("human", "both"):
            print(f"\n[{criterion['id']}] {criterion['description']}")
            verdict = input("PASS or FAIL? ").strip().upper()
            while verdict not in ("PASS", "FAIL"):
                verdict = input("Enter PASS or FAIL: ").strip().upper()
            reason = input("One-sentence reason: ").strip()
            results[criterion["id"]] = {
                "verdict": verdict,
                "reason": reason,
                "tester_id": tester_id,
            }

    return {
        "scenario_id": scenario["id"],
        "timestamp": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        "tester_id": tester_id,
        "response": assistant_response,
        "criteria_results": results,
    }
End-User Tester Protocol

End users should not be told which scenarios they are evaluating — this introduces observer bias. Instead, run them through the application naturally, then ask structured post-session questions for each interaction they had. Capture think-aloud notes rather than scores during the session; scores come after.
Session length: 30–45 minutes per participant; longer sessions fatigue evaluators.
Scenario seeding: pre-configure conversation starters that naturally lead into UAT scenarios without appearing scripted.
Recording: screen + audio recording (with consent) to capture hesitations and re-reads as UX signals.
Post-session survey: 5-point Likert on helpfulness, trust, and task completion per interaction.
Debrief: a 10-minute structured interview surfacing unexpected behaviors that the survey may not capture.
Automated UAT with LLM Judges

Human UAT does not scale to the full scenario library — a team of five testers can realistically evaluate 50–80 scenarios before cognitive fatigue degrades judgment. Automated LLM-judge evaluation handles the remaining scenarios, with human UAT reserved for blocking criteria and new scenario categories.
LLM Judge Runner

# uat_judge.py — automated evaluation using Claude as judge
import anthropic
import json
from pathlib import Path

def evaluate_criterion_automated(
    criterion: dict,
    response: str,
    judge_client: anthropic.Anthropic,
) -> dict:
    judge_prompt = f"""You are a strict UAT evaluator. Evaluate the response below against the acceptance criterion.

Criterion: {criterion['description']}

Positive examples (PASS):
{chr(10).join('  - ' + e for e in criterion.get('positive_examples', []))}

Negative examples (FAIL):
{chr(10).join('  - ' + e for e in criterion.get('negative_examples', []))}

Response to evaluate:
{response}

Respond with exactly:
VERDICT: PASS or FAIL
REASON: one sentence"""

    result = judge_client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=200,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": judge_prompt}],
    )
    text = result.content[0].text.strip()
    verdict_line = next(l for l in text.splitlines() if l.startswith("VERDICT:"))
    reason_line = next(l for l in text.splitlines() if l.startswith("REASON:"))
    return {
        "verdict": "PASS" if "PASS" in verdict_line else "FAIL",
        "reason": reason_line.replace("REASON:", "").strip(),
    }

def run_automated_uat(scenario_path: str) -> dict:
    scenario = json.loads(Path(scenario_path).read_text())
    feature_client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    judge_client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    # Generate response
    messages = [
        {"role": m["role"], "content": m["content"]}
        for m in scenario["turns"]
        if "content" in m
    ]
    response = feature_client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=scenario.get("system_prompt", "You are a helpful assistant."),
        messages=messages,
    ).content[0].text

    # Evaluate automated criteria
    results = {}
    for criterion in scenario["acceptance_criteria"]:
        if criterion["evaluation"] in ("automated", "both"):
            results[criterion["id"]] = evaluate_criterion_automated(
                criterion, response, judge_client
            )

    # Determine overall pass/fail
    blocking_ids = scenario.get("blocking", [])
    blocking_failures = [
        cid for cid in blocking_ids
        if cid in results and results[cid]["verdict"] == "FAIL"
    ]

    return {
        "scenario_id": scenario["id"],
        "response": response,
        "criteria_results": results,
        "blocking_failures": blocking_failures,
        "passed": len(blocking_failures) == 0,
    }
Judge Calibration Against Human Verdicts

Before deploying an automated judge, calibrate it against a set of human-evaluated responses. Run 30 human-evaluated scenarios through the judge and compare verdicts. Target judge-human agreement ≥85% on each criterion. If a criterion has lower agreement, adjust the judge prompt's positive/negative examples until it reaches threshold.
Integrating UAT into the Release Pipeline

UAT produces a release recommendation, not a pass/fail boolean. The recommendation aggregates scores across all scenario categories and presents them alongside a recommended action. The team then makes the release decision based on the recommendation and the specific failures found.
UAT Results Aggregation
UAT CI Job

# .github/workflows/uat.yml
name: UAT Gate

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      release_tag:
        description: Release tag to evaluate
        required: true

jobs:
  automated-uat:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install anthropic pyyaml
      - name: Run automated UAT scenarios
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          python scripts/run_uat.py \
            --scenarios uat/scenarios/ \
            --release ${{ inputs.release_tag }} \
            --output uat-results.json
      - name: Generate UAT report
        run: python scripts/uat_report.py uat-results.json > uat-report.md
      - name: Check for blocking failures
        run: |
          python -c "
          import json, sys
          r = json.load(open('uat-results.json'))
          failures = [s for s in r['scenarios'] if s['blocking_failures']]
          if failures:
              print('BLOCKING FAILURES:')
              for f in failures:
                  print(f'  {f["scenario_id"]}: {f["blocking_failures"]}')
              sys.exit(1)
          print('No blocking failures — automated UAT passed.')
          "
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: uat-report
          path: uat-report.md
Golden Set Management

The golden scenario set is the growing collection of scenarios that have passed human UAT and represent confirmed correct behavior. Each release cycle adds newly validated scenarios to the set. The golden set is replayed automatically on every PR to catch regressions before human UAT is invoked.
Promoting to Golden

# promote_golden.py — promote UAT results to the golden set
import json
from pathlib import Path

GOLDEN_DIR = Path("uat/golden")
SCENARIOS_DIR = Path("uat/scenarios")

def promote_to_golden(scenario_id: str, uat_result: dict) -> None:
    """Add a passed scenario + its response to the golden set."""
    scenario = json.loads((SCENARIOS_DIR / f"{scenario_id}.json").read_text())
    golden_entry = {
        "scenario": scenario,
        "golden_response": uat_result["response"],
        "criteria_results": uat_result["criteria_results"],
        "promoted_at": uat_result["timestamp"],
    }
    golden_path = GOLDEN_DIR / f"{scenario_id}.json"
    golden_path.write_text(json.dumps(golden_entry, indent=2))
    print(f"Promoted {scenario_id} to golden set → {golden_path}")

def replay_golden_set(judge_client) -> dict[str, bool]:
    results = {}
    for golden_file in GOLDEN_DIR.glob("*.json"):
        golden = json.loads(golden_file.read_text())
        # Re-run the scenario and compare against acceptance criteria
        uat_result = run_automated_uat(str(SCENARIOS_DIR / f"{golden['scenario']['id']}.json"))
        results[golden["scenario"]["id"]] = uat_result["passed"]
    return results

Never modify the golden set to make a regression pass. A golden scenario that now fails means the feature changed — either restore the behavior or explicitly retire the golden entry and document why the behavior changed.
Common Pitfalls in Claude UAT
Pitfall
Symptom
Remedy
Testing the model, not the feature
Scenarios test general Claude capability rather than app-specific behavior
Scope scenarios to what your application's system prompt and tool stack enable
Single-turn bias
UAT only tests one-shot exchanges
Add multi-turn conversation flows for every major user journey
Undefined acceptance criteria
Different testers reach opposite verdicts on the same response
Add calibration examples; aim for ≥85% inter-tester agreement before UAT runs
No blocking criteria
UAT produces data but no release gate
Designate at least 3 blocking criteria per feature — security, scope, and accuracy
Golden set rot
Golden set grows stale as app evolves
Review and prune golden entries at each major model or prompt change
Ignoring partial failures
CONDITIONAL recommendation gets silently shipped
Require written exception approval for any CONDITIONAL release
Summary

UAT for Claude features requires adapting traditional acceptance testing to probabilistic, graded outputs. The core adjustments: scenario libraries organized by category and acceptance criteria written as detectable signals rather than exact strings, human testers focused on blocking criteria and new scenario categories with automated LLM judges covering the remainder, calibration of both human testers and automated judges against shared labeled examples, golden set management to catch regressions without re-running human UAT, and a structured recommendation (SHIP / CONDITIONAL / HOLD) that drives the release decision.
Scenario schema: each scenario specifies the conversation, acceptance criteria, evaluation method, and which criteria are blocking.
Criterion pattern: signal-based with positive/negative anchors rather than exact string matching.
LLM judge calibration: target ≥85% agreement with human verdicts before using the judge in CI.
Golden set: grows after each release cycle; replay in CI for regression detection.
Release recommendation: aggregates automated scores + human scores into SHIP / CONDITIONAL / HOLD.
